county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created from Whitman County in 1883. Named after Benjamin Franklin. Pasco became a railroad hub at the confluence of the Snake and Columbia rivers. Part of the Tri-Cities metropolitan area with Kennewick (Benton County) and Richland (Benton County).
county_metadata.research_tips: Franklin County's Pasco was a major Northern Pacific Railroad junction, and railroad employment records can identify workers not found in census records. The county has a significant Hispanic/Latino population dating to early 20th-century agricultural labor, making Spanish-language church records at local Catholic parishes valuable. Before 1883, check records under Whitman County. The Central Regional Branch of the State Archives in Ellensburg holds Franklin County records. The Franklin County Historical Society has pioneer files. Pasco's railroad yards employed diverse workers — check Northern Pacific Railroad personnel records at the Minnesota Historical Society. The Columbia Basin Irrigation Project (post-1952) brought new agricultural settlement. Ice Harbor Dam construction records at the Army Corps of Engineers document workers. FamilySearch has microfilmed early Franklin County marriage records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County Auditor deed records from formation

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed records from county formation including railroad land grants.
Marriage records from county formation. Indexed through Digital Archives.
Federal census from 1890 (destroyed). Railroad workers and agricultural laborers well represented.
Parish records in English and Spanish from Catholic churches serving the Hispanic agricultural community.
Federal homestead patents in the dryland farming area.
Local papers covering railroad activity, irrigation developments, and Tri-Cities growth.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created from Columbia County in 1881. Named after President James A. Garfield. Consistently Washington's least populated county. Located in the Palouse hills of southeastern Washington. Pomeroy is the county seat.
county_metadata.research_tips: Garfield County is Washington's least populated county, which actually makes genealogical research easier because the record set is small and manageable. Nearly everyone knew everyone. The Eastern Regional Branch of the State Archives in Cheney holds Garfield County records. Before 1881, check records under Columbia County, and before 1875 under Walla Walla County. Early settlers were primarily stock raisers and wheat farmers attracted by the Palouse region's rich soil. The Pomeroy area had a significant influx of Civil War veterans who homesteaded under soldier bounty provisions. The Garfield County Museum in Pomeroy has pioneer family files and photographs. FamilySearch has microfilmed early Garfield County vital records. Because the county borders Idaho, some families have records in both Latah and Nez Perce counties, Idaho.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County Auditor deed records from formation; in-person access

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed records from county formation. Small volume makes comprehensive review feasible.
Marriage records from county formation. Very small volume.
Federal census from 1890 (destroyed). Small population. Pre-1881 check Columbia then Walla Walla counties.

source_profiles.repository: Garfield County Auditor / WA State Archives
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Veterans discharge papers filed with county auditor. Many early settlers were Civil War veterans.
Federal homestead patents in the Palouse wheat country.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created from Douglas County in 1909. Named after President Ulysses S. Grant. The Columbia Basin Irrigation Project (water delivered 1952) transformed the county from dryland farming to irrigated agriculture. Moses Lake Army Air Base (later Larson AFB) operated during WWII and the Cold War.
county_metadata.research_tips: Grant County's genealogical records split into pre-irrigation (dryland homesteaders) and post-irrigation (Columbia Basin Project settlers) eras. Before county formation in 1909, check records under Douglas County. The Bureau of Reclamation's Columbia Basin Project records at NARA Denver document water right allotments that effectively served as land grants after 1952. The Central Regional Branch of the State Archives in Ellensburg holds Grant County records. Moses Lake Army Air Base (1942-1966, later Larson AFB) generated military personnel records available through NARA. The Grant County Historical Society in Ephrata has pioneer family files. Japanese-American families from western Washington were interned and some later resettled in the Moses Lake area — check War Relocation Authority records. The Wanapum tribe has traditional ties to Priest Rapids on the Columbia River — Grant County PUD maintains the Wanapum Heritage Center.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County Auditor deed records from formation

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed records from county formation. Major volume increase after Columbia Basin Project (1952).

source_profiles.source_name: Columbia Basin Project Water Rights and Farm Unit Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Farm unit assignments and water right allotments from the Columbia Basin Irrigation Project.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good at NARA Denver and Bureau of Reclamation.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage records from county formation.

source_profiles.source_name: Moses Lake / Larson Air Force Base Records
source_profiles.repository: NARA / Air Force Historical Research Agency
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Personnel and operational records from the WWII training base and Cold War SAC installation.
Federal census from 1910 (first after county formation). Pre-1909 enumerated under Douglas County.

county_metadata.record_losses: No major courthouse fire. Some early Chehalis County territorial records are incomplete.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Originally created as Chehalis County in 1854. Renamed to Grays Harbor County in 1915. Named for the harbor explored by Captain Robert Gray in 1792. Aberdeen and Hoquiam were major timber and fishing towns. Researchers must check records under both Chehalis County (pre-1915) and Grays Harbor County (post-1915).
county_metadata.research_tips: Grays Harbor County was known as Chehalis County until 1915, so researchers must search under both names. Aberdeen and Hoquiam were among the Pacific Northwest's most productive timber ports, and lumber company records (Polson Lumber, Grays Harbor Commercial Company) document thousands of workers. The International Workers of the World (IWW/Wobblies) were active here — the Centralia Massacre (1919) is documented in labor history collections at the University of Washington. The Grays Harbor County Historical Authority in Westport has maritime and fishing industry records. The Quinault Indian Reservation covers the northern coastal portion. Salmon cannery records at NARA Seattle document workers including Chinese, Japanese, and Filipino laborers. The county's Scandinavian fishing community is documented in Nordic Heritage Museum collections. FamilySearch has early Chehalis County marriage records. Aberdeen was the birthplace of Kurt Cobain, and its historical society has broader family research materials.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County Auditor deed records from Chehalis County era

source_profiles.source_name: Grays Harbor County Auditor — Land Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed records from Chehalis County era. Timber company land transactions prominent.

source_profiles.source_name: Grays Harbor / Chehalis County Marriage Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage records under both Chehalis County and Grays Harbor County names.

source_profiles.source_name: Grays Harbor Salmon Cannery Worker Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Records of Chinese, Japanese, and Filipino cannery workers at Grays Harbor salmon processing facilities.
BIA records for the Quinault Reservation. Census rolls, allotment records, and treaty files.
Enumerated as Chehalis County through 1910, Grays Harbor County from 1920. Timber workers and fishermen prominent.

source_profiles.source_name: Aberdeen Daily World and Harbor Newspapers
source_profiles.repository: Chronicling America / Grays Harbor Historical Authority
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Aberdeen and Hoquiam newspapers. Strong timber industry and labor movement coverage.

county_metadata.record_losses: No major record losses. Records well preserved.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created as one of Washington Territory's first counties in 1853. Consists of Whidbey Island, Camano Island, and smaller islands. Coupeville is the county seat and one of Washington's oldest towns. Whidbey Island Naval Air Station has been a major employer since 1942. Ebey's Landing National Historical Reserve preserves the agricultural landscape.
county_metadata.research_tips: Island County has some of Washington's oldest continuous settlement records because Whidbey Island attracted Donation Land Claim settlers in the early 1850s. Ebey's Landing National Historical Reserve preserves documentation of these pioneer families. Captain Thomas Ebey was killed by Northern Indians in 1857, and the event is well documented in territorial records. The Northwest Regional Branch of the State Archives in Bellingham holds Island County records. Coupeville, one of the oldest towns in Washington, has records from the 1850s at the Island County Historical Museum. Whidbey Island Naval Air Station (established 1942) generated military personnel records. The Dutch and Scandinavian farming communities on Whidbey and Camano islands are documented in local church records. FamilySearch has microfilmed early vital records. Deception Pass connected the county to Skagit County and many families had cross-county connections.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County Auditor deed records from early territorial era

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed records from earliest territorial period. Includes Donation Land Claims from the 1850s.
DLCs from early Whidbey Island settlement. Critical for identifying pioneer families and their wives.
Marriage records from territorial era. Indexed through Digital Archives.

source_profiles.source_name: Whidbey Island Naval Air Station Records
source_profiles.repository: NARA / Naval History and Heritage Command
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Personnel and operational records from NAS Whidbey Island.
Federal census from 1860. Small island population makes comprehensive review manageable.

source_profiles.repository: Island County Historical Museum / Find A Grave
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Pioneer cemetery transcriptions from Whidbey and Camano islands including Sunnyside Cemetery in Coupeville.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Well documented by local historical societies.

county_metadata.parent_county: Original territorial county (from Lewis County under Oregon Territory)
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created under Oregon Territory in 1852, before Washington Territory was organized. Named after President Thomas Jefferson. Port Townsend was the major customs port of entry for Puget Sound and was once considered a rival to Seattle. Olympic National Park and Forest cover much of the county.
county_metadata.research_tips: Jefferson County's Port Townsend was Puget Sound's official port of entry, making its customs records uniquely valuable for tracking maritime arrivals. US Customs passenger and crew manifests at NARA Seattle document ships arriving from around the Pacific. Port Townsend's Victorian architecture reflects its 1880s boom when it was expected to become the terminus of the transcontinental railroad. The Jefferson County Historical Society has exceptional collections in the restored city hall. County records date from 1852, predating Washington Territory itself. Fort Worden military records (1902-1953) at NARA document coastal defense garrison personnel. The county's waterfront Chinese community — pushed out in the 1880s anti-Chinese violence — is documented in customs records and city council minutes. FamilySearch has microfilmed early marriage records. The county seat never changed, so records have remained in one location continuously since the 1850s.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County Auditor deed records from pre-territorial era

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed records from pre-territorial era. Port Townsend property records among oldest in Washington.
Customs passenger and crew manifests for vessels entering Puget Sound through Port Townsend. The primary port of entry for the region.
Marriage records from earliest territorial period. Indexed through Digital Archives.
Post returns and personnel records from Fort Worden coastal defense installation.
Federal census from 1860. Port Townsend shows diverse maritime population including sailors, customs officials, and Chinese merchants.

county_metadata.parent_county: Original territorial county (from Thurston under Oregon Territory)
county_metadata.record_losses: The Great Seattle Fire of 1889 destroyed much of the central business district but courthouse records survived. Some early city records were lost.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created under Oregon Territory in 1852. Named after William Rufus DeVane King, Vice President under Franklin Pierce. King County is the most populous county in Washington and home to Seattle. The county was later rededicated in honor of Martin Luther King Jr. in 2005. Seattle became the major port of the Pacific Northwest.
county_metadata.research_tips: King County (Seattle) has the most extensive genealogical resources in Washington state. The Seattle Municipal Archives holds city records from the 1860s. The Great Fire of 1889 destroyed parts of downtown but courthouse records survived because the courthouse was on higher ground. The Museum of History and Industry (MOHAI) has photograph and manuscript collections. The University of Washington Special Collections holds labor, ethnic community, and Pacific Northwest history collections. For Chinese and Japanese community research, the Wing Luke Museum archives are essential. The Klondike Gold Rush National Historical Park in Pioneer Square documents the 1897-1898 outfitting trade. Boeing company employment records (from 1916) document tens of thousands of workers. Seattle's Chinatown-International District has records at the Wing Luke Museum. Densho.org provides free access to Japanese American incarceration records. FamilySearch has extensive King County microfilm. Check both county and city records as Seattle maintained separate vital statistics.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: King County Recording Division; online index and images available

source_profiles.source_name: King County Recording Division — Land Records
source_profiles.repository: King County Dept of Records, Seattle
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Comprehensive deed records with online index. Includes plat maps and recording documents.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Survived 1889 fire. Complete from territorial era.

source_profiles.repository: WA Digital Archives / King County Archives
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage records from territorial era. Both county and city records available.

source_profiles.source_name: Seattle Chinese Exclusion Act Case Files
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Individual case files with photographs, witness testimony, and family information for Chinese residents.
Declarations of intention and petitions from King County courts. Cover diverse immigrant communities — Scandinavian, Chinese, Japanese, Filipino, Italian.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — many indexed at Digital Archives.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census from 1860. Seattle shows rapid growth and ethnic diversity.
Major regional newspapers. Obituaries, legal notices, shipping news, and community coverage.

source_profiles.repository: WA Digital Archives / King County Archives
source_profiles.coverage_notes: City of Seattle maintained separate death records. State registration from 1907.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — Seattle city records supplement state records.

county_metadata.parent_county: King and Jefferson (originally as Slaughter County)
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Originally created as Slaughter County in 1857. Renamed to Kitsap County in 1893 after Chief Kitsap of the Suquamish tribe. Home to Puget Sound Naval Shipyard in Bremerton, one of the largest naval installations on the West Coast. The Suquamish and Port Madison Indian Reservations are within the county.
county_metadata.research_tips: Kitsap County's identity centers on the Puget Sound Naval Shipyard in Bremerton (established 1891), which has employed tens of thousands of workers. Navy Yard employment records at NARA are crucial for tracking families. The county was originally called Slaughter County (1857-1893), so early records use that name. Chief Seattle (Si'ahl) of the Suquamish died and is buried at the Port Madison Reservation — the Suquamish Museum has tribal genealogical resources. The county's proximity to Seattle via ferry means many families appear in both King and Kitsap county records. Port Orchard and Bremerton city directories can supplement census records. The Naval Undersea Warfare Center at Keyport also generated personnel records. FamilySearch has microfilmed Kitsap County vital records. Scandinavian fishermen and loggers settled Poulsbo and other waterfront communities — check Norwegian-language church records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County Auditor recording division; records from territorial era

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed records from formation as Slaughter County. Includes naval yard area properties.

source_profiles.source_name: Puget Sound Naval Shipyard Employment Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Employment records for one of the largest naval installations on the West Coast. Thousands of civilian workers documented.
Marriage records from territorial era. Filed under Slaughter County before 1893.

source_profiles.source_name: Tulalip / Puget Sound Agency — Suquamish Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: BIA records for Suquamish and Port Madison Reservation. Census rolls and allotment records.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good at NARA Seattle. Suquamish Museum has supplementary materials.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Enumerated as Slaughter County through 1890, Kitsap County from 1900. Note naval base population.

source_profiles.source_name: Bremerton Sun and Kitsap County Papers
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Local newspapers with strong naval shipyard and military community coverage.

county_metadata.record_losses: The great Ellensburg fire of July 4, 1889 destroyed most of the downtown business district, but courthouse records largely survived as the courthouse was rebuilt.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created from Yakima County in 1883. Named from a Yakama word meaning 'shoal people.' Ellensburg was a contender for state capital in 1889. Home to Central Washington University and the Washington State Archives Central Regional Branch. The county spans from Cascade passes to sagebrush country.
county_metadata.research_tips: Kittitas County's Ellensburg hosts the Central Regional Branch of the Washington State Archives, making it a research hub for central Washington counties (Benton, Chelan, Douglas, Franklin, Grant, Kittitas, Klickitat, Okanogan, Walla Walla, and Yakima). The 1889 Ellensburg fire destroyed downtown but the county rebuilt and most records survived. Before 1883, check records under Yakima County. The county's economy blended ranching, mining (Cle Elum coal mines), and agriculture. The Northern Pacific Railroad brought Italian coal miners to Cle Elum and Roslyn — the Roslyn cemetery has an unusual ethnic section layout documenting distinct immigrant communities. The Kittitas County Historical Museum has pioneer family files. Central Washington University archives hold regional oral histories. FamilySearch has microfilmed Kittitas County vital records. The county's high Cascade passes (Snoqualmie, Stampede) were key transportation routes.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County Auditor recording records from formation

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed records from county formation. Includes ranch land, coal mining claims, and town lots.

source_profiles.repository: WA Digital Archives / WA State Archives Central Branch
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage records from county formation. Reflect multi-ethnic mining community.

source_profiles.source_name: Roslyn and Cle Elum Coal Mining Death Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Death records documenting mine accidents and occupational deaths in the coal mining communities of Roslyn, Cle Elum, and Ronald.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good from state registration (1907). Earlier county records partial.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census from 1890 (destroyed). Roslyn/Cle Elum shows Italian, Slavic, and Black coal miners.

source_profiles.repository: Roslyn Historical Museum / Find A Grave
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Roslyn's 26 ethnic lodge cemeteries document Italian, Croatian, Lithuanian, Polish, Black, and other mining communities.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Well preserved — unique ethnic section layout.

source_profiles.source_name: Ellensburg Capital and Cle Elum Echo
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Local newspapers covering ranching, mining, and university community news.
